evals.prompt: Write a stack data structure in Python. It should support push, pop, and peek. Pop and peek on an empty stack should raise an error.
evals.expected_output: A Stack class with tests covering empty, one-item, and multi-item cases, built test-first with minimal code at each step
expectations: Claude announces using TDD skill with mode at the start
Test plan is written as [TEST] comments before any implementation
ZOMBIES checklist is explicitly walked through to verify test completeness
Tests are written one at a time, not in batches
Each test is in given-when-then format with empty lines separating sections
Claude predicts the failure before running each test
The two-step red phase is followed: first compilation error, then assertion failure
Only minimal code is added to make each test pass — no speculative implementation
All tests are run after each change, not just the current one
A refactoring stage occurs after tests go green, announced with the refactoring emoji
Production code contains no comments
Final evaluation checks for missing tests and hardcoded values

evals.prompt: I need a function that converts Roman numerals to integers in TypeScript. Handle I through M, including subtractive notation like IV, IX, XL, etc.
evals.expected_output: A roman numeral converter built incrementally through TDD, starting with simplest cases and building up
expectations: Claude announces using TDD skill with mode at the start
Test plan starts with the simplest case (single numeral) before complex ones
Tests progress from zero/one cases to many to boundary cases following ZOMBIES
Subtractive notation (IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM) is covered in the test plan
Each test is written before the code that makes it pass
Claude predicts failures before running tests
Implementation grows incrementally — no big-bang solution dropped in
Refactoring happens between test cycles when opportunities arise
Invalid input handling is deferred until happy paths work (ZOMBIES E-last principle)
Final code has no comments

evals.prompt: Build a simple shopping cart in Java. It should add items with a name and price, calculate the total, and apply a percentage discount. Don't worry about persistence.
evals.expected_output: A ShoppingCart class developed through strict TDD with comprehensive test coverage
expectations: Claude announces using TDD skill with mode at the start
Test plan covers empty cart, single item, multiple items, and discount edge cases
The simplest test is tackled first (empty cart total is zero or similar)
Claude does not implement discount logic until a test demands it
Two-step red phase is visible: compilation error then assertion failure
Code is simplified after each green — Claude asks 'does a failing test require this?'
Domain concepts emerge through refactoring, not upfront design
All tests run after every change
Final evaluation reviews for missing edge cases

evals.prompt: Build a tennis game scorer in Python. Track points for two players. Use traditional tennis scoring (0, 15, 30, 40). Handle deuce (both at 40) and advantage rules. The game ends when a player wins. Provide a method that returns the current score as a string like '15-30' or 'Deuce' or 'Advantage Player 1' or 'Player 2 wins'.
evals.expected_output: A tennis scorer built through TDD with tests covering normal scoring, deuce, advantage, and win conditions
expectations: Test plan covers normal scoring progression before deuce/advantage scenarios
ZOMBIES is walked through explicitly — zero (initial score), one (first point), many (multiple points), boundary (40-40 transition to deuce), exceptions (scoring after game is won)
Deuce logic is NOT implemented until a test forces it — early tests use hardcoded or simple score tracking
The transition from normal scoring to deuce scoring emerges through tests, not upfront design
Advantage and win-from-advantage are separate test cases, not lumped together
Claude predicts failures before running each test
A meaningful refactoring occurs — the scoring logic should evolve from simple to complex as tests demand it
Scoring after a game is won is handled (either raises error or is tested)
Final code correctly handles: normal points, deuce, advantage, win from advantage, win from 40-0/15/30
All tests run after every change
